Tool call 3 — open widgets.py
I will open lib/matplotlib/widgets.py to read the RangeSlider class and SliderBase, Slider for context, and the RangeSlider.set_val method.

Result analysis (summary of what I inspected):
- The RangeSlider.__init__ creates self.poly via ax.axvspan or ax.axhspan:
  - For horizontal orientation: self.poly = ax.axvspan(valinit[0], valinit[1], 0, 1, **kwargs)
  - For vertical: self.poly = ax.axhspan(...)
- RangeSlider.set_val (current code in repository) sorts the val array, checks its shape, bounds the min/max, then obtains xy = self.poly.xy and mutates entries:
  - For vertical:
      xy[0] = (.25, val[0])
      xy[1] = (.25, val[1])
      xy[2] = (.75, val[1])
      xy[3] = (.75, val[0])
      xy[4] = (.25, val[0])
  - For horizontal: similar with x/y swapped, and also sets xy[4] = val[0], .25
- The code therefore expects poly.xy to have at least 5 vertices (indices 0..4).
- In the reporter's environment poly.xy had shape (4,2), so xy[4] caused IndexError.

Tool call 4 — inspect axvspan implementation and Polygon
I will open lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py (axvspan/axhspan) and lib/matplotlib/patches.py (Polygon) to see how the polygon vertices are set and whether the closing vertex is present.

Result analysis:
- axes._axes.axvspan constructs verts as four 2-tuples:
    verts = [(xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin)]
  then:
    p = mpatches.Polygon(verts, **kwargs)
- patches.Polygon.__init__ sets self._closed = closed (default closed=True), then calls set_xy(xy).
- patches.Polygon.set_xy converts xy to an ndarray; if self._closed True and first != last, it appends the first vertex to close the polygon:
    if self._closed:
        if nverts == 1 or (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any():
            xy = np.concatenate([xy, [xy[0]]])
- get_xy returns self._path.vertices.

Interpretation:
- When a Polygon is constructed with the 4-vertex list and closed=True, set_xy should append the first vertex, so self.poly.xy should normally have 5 vertices (the last equal to the first).
- However, it is possible for poly.xy to be recorded as 4 vertices in some circumstances (for example, if the Polygon's closed attribute is False or if there are differences in older/newer versions or transformations). In any case, directly indexing xy[4] is brittle.

Root cause
- RangeSlider.set_val assumes self.poly.xy has 5 vertices and assigns to xy[4]. If poly.xy only has 4 vertices (shape (4,2)), indexing xy[4] raises IndexError.
- The code attempts to update the polygon's vertices in-place by mutating xy indices; it should not assume the exact number of vertices (4 vs 5) — better to construct the desired vertex array from the values and set self.poly.xy to that array. This is robust whether Polygon automatically appends a closing vertex or not.

Complete fix (what needs to change)
- File to change:
  - lib/matplotlib/widgets.py — method RangeSlider.set_val

- Change summary:
  - Replace the in-place mutation of self.poly.xy (which assumes 5 vertices) with building the 4 core vertices (the rectangle corners in the correct order for orientation) and assigning self.poly.xy = new_xy. Let Polygon.set_xy handle whether it should append the closing vertex (it will if Polygon is closed). That handles both cases (existing poly.xy has 4 or 5 vertices) and avoids IndexError.

- Exact change (diff-style description):
  - Replace the body of RangeSlider.set_val (the part that mutates xy) with code that builds a 4x2 array of vertices and assigns self.poly.xy = base. Keep the rest of the method (update valtext, draw, self.val, notify observers) intact.

- Why this is complete:
  - Only RangeSlider.set_val assumed a 5th vertex. Slider.set_val accesses indices that are always present for both 4- and 5-vertex polygons (it only used indices up to 3), so no other place needs a change.
  - The new approach is robust to polygon closed/unclosed shapes and to different implementations that produce 4 or 5 vertices.

Patch (proposed implementation)
- Current code (excerpt):
    def set_val(self, val):
        val = np.sort(val)
        _api.check_shape((2,), val=val)
        val[0] = self._min_in_bounds(val[0])
        val[1] = self._max_in_bounds(val[1])
        xy = self.poly.xy
        if self.orientation == "vertical":
            xy[0] = .25, val[0]
            xy[1] = .25, val[1]
            xy[2] = .75, val[1]
            xy[3] = .75, val[0]
            xy[4] = .25, val[0]
        else:
            xy[0] = val[0], .25
            xy[1] = val[0], .75
            xy[2] = val[1], .75
            xy[3] = val[1], .25
            xy[4] = val[0], .25
        self.poly.xy = xy
        ...

- Replace with (safe, robust implementation):
    def set_val(self, val):
        val = np.sort(np.asanyarray(val))
        _api.check_shape((2,), val=val)
        val[0] = self._min_in_bounds(val[0])
        val[1] = self._max_in_bounds(val[1])

        # Build the 4 core vertices for the rectangular span. If the
        # underlying Polygon is closed, set_xy will append the closing
        # vertex; if not, set_xy will keep the 4 vertices.
        if self.orientation == "vertical":
            # Points ordered (x,y): left-bottom, left-top, right-top, right-bottom
            base = np.array([[.25, val[0]],
                             [.25, val[1]],
                             [.75, val[1]],
                             [.75, val[0]]])
        else:
            # Points ordered (x,y): left-bottom, left-top, right-top, right-bottom
            base = np.array([[val[0], .25],
                             [val[0], .75],
                             [val[1], .75],
                             [val[1], .25]])

        # Assign vertices; Polygon.set_xy will append the closing vertex
        # if the polygon is configured to be closed.
        self.poly.xy = base

        self.valtext.set_text(self._format(val))
        if self.drawon:
            self.ax.figure.canvas.draw_idle()
        self.val = val
        if self.eventson:
            self._observers.process("changed", val)

Notes and rationale
- Using np.asanyarray and np.sort ensures we handle sequences and numpy arrays consistently and keep the previous behavior of sorting/sanitizing values.
- Building a fresh base array is less error-prone than mutating xy in place and avoids indexing failures when the polygon has either 4 or 5 vertices.
- Letting Polygon.set_xy handle whether to append a close vertex preserves the polygon's closed/unclosed semantics and avoids duplicating that logic.

Other locations to consider
- Slider.set_val mutates self.poly.xy by indexing up to xy[3]; it is safe for both 4- and 5-vertex polygons (indices 0..3 exist). No change required there.
- RangeSlider.__init__ constructs the polygon via ax.axvspan/axhspan. We do not change its polygon creation; the fix only makes set_val robust to the actual polygon vertex representation.
- No constants need to become dynamic.

Testing suggestions
- Re-run the reporter's minimal reproduction with valinit=[0.0, 0.0] (the failing case).
- Test both horizontal and vertical RangeSlider usage.
- Test with kwargs that might set closed=False when creating the polygon to ensure set_val still works (Polygon.set_xy will not append closing vertex and the base 4 vertices assignment is correct).
- Run the widget tests (if available) that exercise RangeSlider behavior.

Why this fixes the user's error
- The user's IndexError occurs when self.poly.xy has only 4 vertices; writing to xy[4] raises. By assigning the 4 core vertices (and allowing Polygon.set_xy to add the closing vertex if configured), the method avoids indexing element 4 and therefore avoids IndexError. This preserves the intended rectangle shape and the slider functionality.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a proper patch/diff for lib/matplotlib/widgets.py (RangeSlider.set_val) in unified diff format.
- Run a small test script (local simulation) to demonstrate before/after behavior.

bug_report.Title: RangeSlider.set_val IndexError when polygon has 4 vertices (cannot initialize RangeSlider)
bug_report.Description: Creating a RangeSlider with valinit triggers an IndexError in RangeSlider.set_val because the implementation assumes the polygon (self.poly) has 5 vertices and writes to xy[4], but in some environments self.poly.xy has only 4 vertices.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) RangeSlider.set_val in lib/matplotlib/widgets.py assumes self.poly.xy has 5 vertices and assigns to xy[4]. Location: RangeSlider.set_val (widgets.py). The code uses index-based in-place mutation of the polygon vertex array and expects a closing vertex to already be present.
2) The assumption that self.poly.xy always contains the appended closing vertex is incorrect. The polygon returned by axvspan/axhspan may be represented with 4 vertices (the rectangle corners) or with 5 vertices (the closing first vertex repeated). The correct relationship is that the method should consider the 4 core rectangle vertices (ordered corners) and not rely on the presence of a fifth element; constructing the 4 core vertices and assigning them to self.poly.xy is correct because Polygon.set_xy will append the closing vertex if the Polygon is configured to be closed.
3) The buggy code mutates indices up to 4 (xy[4]) which is only valid if poly.xy already contains the closing point; when poly.xy has shape (4,2) this causes IndexError: index 4 is out of bounds. Location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py: RangeSlider.set_val (the block that sets xy[0]..xy[4]).
StepsToReproduce: Run the provided reproduction script (the one creating an image, histogram, and a RangeSlider with valinit=[0.0, 0.0]).
Observe the IndexError raised during RangeSlider construction (stack trace points to widgets.py RangeSlider.set_val and xy[4]).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: RangeSlider should accept and apply the provided initial value (valinit) and render the rectangular span on creation without raising an IndexError, updating the image/histogram accordingly.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Creating RangeSlider with valinit triggers an IndexError: 'index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4' coming from RangeSlider.set_val when it attempts to write to xy[4].

def process_value(value):
        """
        Homogenize the input *value* for easy and efficient normalization.

        *value* can be a scalar or sequence.

        Returns
        -------
        result : masked array
            Masked array with the same shape as *value*.
        is_scalar : bool
            Whether *value* is a scalar.

        Notes
        -----
        Float dtypes are preserved; integer types with two bytes or smaller are
        converted to np.float32, and larger types are converted to np.float64.
        Preserving float32 when possible, and using in-place operations,
        greatly improves speed for large arrays.
        """
        is_scalar = not np.iterable(value)
        if is_scalar:
            value = [value]
        dtype = np.min_scalar_type(value)
        if np.issubdtype(dtype, np.integer) or dtype.type is np.bool_:
            # bool_/int8/int16 -> float32; int32/int64 -> float64
            dtype = np.promote_types(dtype, np.float32)
        # ensure data passed in as an ndarray subclass are interpreted as
        # an ndarray. See issue #6622.
        mask = np.ma.getmask(value)
        data = np.asarray(value)
        result = np.ma.array(data, mask=mask, dtype=dtype, copy=True)
        return result, is_scalar

    def __call__(self, value, clip=None):
        """
        Normalize *value* data in the ``[vmin, vmax]`` interval into the
        ``[0.0, 1.0]`` interval and return it.

        Parameters
        ----------
        value
            Data to normalize.
        clip : bool
            If ``None``, defaults to ``self.clip`` (which defaults to
            ``False``).

        Notes
        -----
        If not already initialized, ``self.vmin`` and ``self.vmax`` are
        initialized using ``self.autoscale_None(value)``.
        """
        if clip is None:
            clip = self.clip

        result, is_scalar = self.process_value(value)

        if self.vmin is None or self.vmax is None:
            self.autoscale_None(result)
        # Convert at least to float, without losing precision.
        (vmin,), _ = self.process_value(self.vmin)
        (vmax,), _ = self.process_value(self.vmax)
        if vmin == vmax:
            result.fill(0)  # Or should it be all masked?  Or 0.5?
        elif vmin > vmax:
            raise ValueError("minvalue must be less than or equal to maxvalue")
        else:
            if clip:
                mask = np.ma.getmask(result)
                result = np.ma.array(np.clip(result.filled(vmax), vmin, vmax),
                                     mask=mask)
            # ma division is very slow; we can take a shortcut
            resdat = result.data
            resdat -= vmin
            resdat /= (vmax - vmin)
            result = np.ma.array(resdat, mask=result.mask, copy=False)
        if is_scalar:
            result = result[0]
        return result

    def inverse(self, value):
        if not self.scaled():
            raise ValueError("Not invertible until both vmin and vmax are set")
        (vmin,), _ = self.process_value(self.vmin)
        (vmax,), _ = self.process_value(self.vmax)

        if np.iterable(value):
            val = np.ma.asarray(value)
            return vmin + val * (vmax - vmin)
        else:
            return vmin + value * (vmax - vmin)

    def autoscale(self, A):
        """Set *vmin*, *vmax* to min, max of *A*."""
        A = np.asanyarray(A)
        self.vmin = A.min()
        self.vmax = A.max()

    def autoscale_None(self, A):
        """If vmin or vmax are not set, use the min/max of *A* to set them."""
        A = np.asanyarray(A)
        if self.vmin is None and A.size:
            self.vmin = A.min()
        if self.vmax is None and A.size:
            self.vmax = A.max()

    def scaled(self):
        """Return whether vmin and vmax are set."""
        return self.vmin is not None and self.vmax is not None

        """
        Normalize data with a set center.

        Useful when mapping data with an unequal rates of change around a
        conceptual center, e.g., data that range from -2 to 4, with 0 as
        the midpoint.

        Parameters
        ----------
        vcenter : float
            The data value that defines ``0.5`` in the normalization.
        vmin : float, optional
            The data value that defines ``0.0`` in the normalization.
            Defaults to the min value of the dataset.
        vmax : float, optional
            The data value that defines ``1.0`` in the normalization.
            Defaults to the max value of the dataset.

        --------
        This maps data value -4000 to 0., 0 to 0.5, and +10000 to 1.0; data
        between is linearly interpolated::

            >>> import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
            >>> offset = mcolors.TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=-4000.,
                                              vcenter=0., vmax=10000)
            >>> data = [-4000., -2000., 0., 2500., 5000., 7500., 10000.]
            >>> offset(data)

    def _get_extension_lengths(self, frac, automin, automax, default=0.05):
        """
        Return the lengths of colorbar extensions.

        This is a helper method for _uniform_y and _proportional_y.
        """
        # Set the default value.
        extendlength = np.array([default, default])
        if isinstance(frac, str):
            _api.check_in_list(['auto'], extendfrac=frac.lower())
            # Use the provided values when 'auto' is required.
            extendlength[:] = [automin, automax]
        elif frac is not None:
            try:
                # Try to set min and max extension fractions directly.
                extendlength[:] = frac
                # If frac is a sequence containing None then NaN may
                # be encountered. This is an error.
                if np.isnan(extendlength).any():
                    raise ValueError()
            except (TypeError, ValueError) as err:
                # Raise an error on encountering an invalid value for frac.
                raise ValueError('invalid value for extendfrac') from err
        return extendlength

    def _extend_lower(self):
        """Return whether the lower limit is open ended."""
        minmax = "max" if self._long_axis().get_inverted() else "min"
        return self.extend in ('both', minmax)

    def _extend_upper(self):
        """Return whether the upper limit is open ended."""
        minmax = "min" if self._long_axis().get_inverted() else "max"
        return self.extend in ('both', minmax)

Thought: Plan / starting point
- The stack trace shows the crash originates in Artist.format_cursor_data (lib/matplotlib/artist.py) when it calls self.norm.inverse(...). The norm in question is BoundaryNorm, and BoundaryNorm.inverse always raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible") (lib/matplotlib/colors.py).
- I will inspect Artist.format_cursor_data and BoundaryNorm to confirm behavior and decide the least-invasive, correct fix.

What I inspected (tool log)
- Located and read Artist.format_cursor_data (lib/matplotlib/artist.py). It attempts to compute "neighbors" by calling self.norm.inverse(...) to find the data-space boundaries corresponding to neighboring color intervals, and then uses that to compute the number of significant digits for formatting the scalar value shown in the status bar.
- Located and read BoundaryNorm (lib/matplotlib/colors.py). BoundaryNorm.__call__ maps data to integer color indices; BoundaryNorm.inverse explicitly raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible").
- Reviewed colorbar code (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py) to confirm other call sites of norm.inverse already special-case BoundaryNorm; i.e., the colorbar code handles BoundaryNorm without calling inverse.

Root cause
- Artist.format_cursor_data assumes that self.norm.inverse(...) will succeed. For BoundaryNorm, inverse() raises ValueError unconditionally, which percolates up and crashes interaction when the cursor hovers over an image whose norm is a BoundaryNorm.
- This is a real bug (crash on mouseover) and the minimally invasive fix is to protect format_cursor_data against norms that are not invertible, rather than changing BoundaryNorm.inverse contract (which may be relied on elsewhere).

Design of fix
- Wrap the self.norm.inverse(...) call in try/except ValueError (the exception BoundaryNorm raises). On catching ValueError, compute a reasonable fallback "neighbors" in data-space so the rest of the formatting code can proceed.
- Preferred fallback behavior:
  - If the norm has a boundaries attribute (BoundaryNorm), use the boundaries to find the lower and upper edges of the bin containing the scalar data, and use those edges as neighbors. This yields a sensible delta for significant-digit calculation.
  - If there are no boundaries or anything else goes wrong, fall back to a tiny +/- epsilon around the data value (using vmin/vmax if available to scale eps), so we still compute a reasonable number of significant digits and avoid crash.
- This change is localized to Artist.format_cursor_data. Other code paths (colorbar, etc.) already special-case BoundaryNorm and do not need to be changed.

Exact change (code)
- File: lib/matplotlib/artist.py
- Replace the block (current lines ~1301-1313):

    n = self.cmap.N
    if np.ma.getmask(data):
        return "[]"
    normed = self.norm(data)
    if np.isfinite(normed):
        # Midpoints of neighboring color intervals.
        neighbors = self.norm.inverse(
            (int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
        delta = abs(neighbors - data).max()
        g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta)
    else:
        g_sig_digits = 3  # Consistent with default below.
    return "[{:-#.{}g}]".format(data, g_sig_digits)

- With the following guarded implementation:

    n = self.cmap.N
    if np.ma.getmask(data):
        return "[]"
    normed = self.norm(data)
    if np.isfinite(normed):
        # Midpoints of neighboring color intervals. Attempt to invert the
        # normalized intervals back to data-space; if the norm is not
        # invertible (e.g., BoundaryNorm), fall back to using the
        # norm.boundaries (if present) or a small epsilon around *data*.
        try:
            neighbors = self.norm.inverse(
                (int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
        except ValueError:
            # Norm not invertible. Try to use 'boundaries' (BoundaryNorm).
            b = getattr(self.norm, "boundaries", None)
            if b is not None:
                # Find the bin containing *data* and use its edges.
                try:
                    idx = np.digitize([data], b)[0] - 1
                except Exception:
                    idx = 0
                idx0 = max(0, idx)
                idx1 = min(len(b) - 1, idx0 + 1)
                neighbors = np.array([b[idx0], b[idx1]], dtype=float)
            else:
                # Last-resort fallback: small epsilon around data, scaled by
                # vmin/vmax if available, or relative to data if nonzero.
                vmin = getattr(self.norm, "vmin", None)
                vmax = getattr(self.norm, "vmax", None)
                if (vmin is None or vmax is None or
                        not np.isfinite(vmin) or not np.isfinite(vmax) or
                        (vmax - vmin) == 0):
                    eps = abs(data) * 1e-6 if data != 0 else 1e-6
                else:
                    eps = (vmax - vmin) / max(n, 1) / 2.0
                neighbors = np.array([data - eps, data + eps], dtype=float)
        delta = abs(neighbors - data).max()
        g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta)
    else:
        g_sig_digits = 3  # Consistent with default below.
    return "[{:-#.{}g}]".format(data, g_sig_digits)

Rationale for this exact approach
- It avoids changing the public behavior of BoundaryNorm (which explicitly documents that it is not invertible).
- It makes Artist.format_cursor_data robust: it will no longer crash when a norm's inverse is unavailable. The purpose of calling inverse here is only to compute a delta used to set the number of significant digits — it's acceptable to use slightly approximate neighbors.
- Using boundaries when available for BoundaryNorm gives meaningful, expected deltas (the data-space range of the color bin containing the value).
- The fallback eps heuristic is conservative and only used if boundaries are not available.

Other locations considered
- colorbar.py already special-cases BoundaryNorm and NoNorm to avoid calling inverse(). No changes necessary there.
- I searched for other call-sites of norm.inverse in the codebase. They either are in contexts that already guard against BoundaryNorm or expect invertibility (e.g., plain Normalize or certain scale-based Norms). Changing BoundaryNorm.inverse to something approximating inversion is possible, but:
  - It would alter BoundaryNorm's documented contract ("not invertible") and could create subtle semantic changes elsewhere.
  - The single robust fix is to make the client (Artist.format_cursor_data) tolerant of a non-invertible norm.

Tests / verification
- Reproduce the original example:

    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    import numpy as np
    import matplotlib as mpl
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    norm = mpl.colors.BoundaryNorm(np.linspace(-4, 4, 5), 256)
    X = np.random.randn(10, 10)
    pc = ax.imshow(X, cmap='RdBu_r', norm=norm)
    # Move mouse over image; no crash should occur.

- Add unit tests:
  - Test that format_cursor_data([scalar]) on an Artist/ScalarMappable with a BoundaryNorm returns a string and does not raise.
  - Test that when norm is invertible (Normalize, LogNorm, etc.) format_cursor_data behavior is unchanged.

Notes / alternatives
- Alternative: make BoundaryNorm.inverse do an approximate inverse (e.g., map normalized numbers in [0,1] to boundaries or interpolations). That would allow existing callers of inverse to work. However:
  - It changes a documented contract and could hide errors where callers rely on a true continuous inverse.
  - Many code paths (colorbar) already special-case BoundaryNorm, so a change there is not strictly necessary.
- The chosen fix is:
  - minimal (single-file change)
  - safe (no change to public API of Norms)
  - correct for the use-case (formatting cursor data)

Complete list of files to change
- lib/matplotlib/artist.py
  - Change only the format_cursor_data method as described above.

Why this is a complete fix
- The crash happened only because format_cursor_data did not handle the ValueError raised by BoundaryNorm.inverse. No other sites require editing.
- By adding the try/except and a reasonable fallback (prefer boundaries when available), the intended functionality (formatting the scalar under the cursor) will continue to work without crashing for BoundaryNorm (and for any other norm that might raise ValueError on inverse), while leaving other norm behavior intact.

If you want, I can prepare a patch/diff for lib/matplotlib/artist.py containing the exact replacement lines (with correct indentation and imports already present in the file).

bug_report.Title: Hovering over image with BoundaryNorm crashes (ValueError: BoundaryNorm is not invertible)
bug_report.Description: When an AxesImage / ScalarMappable is given a BoundaryNorm, moving the mouse over the image triggers Artist.format_cursor_data which calls the norm's inverse; BoundaryNorm.inverse raises ValueError and the exception is unhandled, causing the interactive cursor behavior to crash.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.format_cursor_data assumes the norm is invertible and unconditionally calls:
   neighbors = self.norm.inverse((int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
   The subsequent logic computes
   delta = abs(neighbors - data).max()
   g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta)
   This call-site does not catch ValueError and therefore lets any ValueError from norm.inverse propagate.

2) lib/matplotlib/colors.py::BoundaryNorm.inverse intentionally raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible"). BoundaryNorm therefore does not provide an inverse; the contract is explicit in this implementation.

3) Because format_cursor_data relies on norm.inverse only to derive neighboring data-space values (to compute a delta for digit formatting), the unhandled ValueError from a non-invertible norm (BoundaryNorm) causes interaction to stop rather than falling back to a safe formatting strategy.

4) The rest of the codebase (e.g., colorbar) already special-cases BoundaryNorm, indicating the assumption of invertibility is not globally valid; the failing assumption is localized to Artist.format_cursor_data.
StepsToReproduce:
Run the following Python script with an interactive Matplotlib backend (e.g. Qt5Agg):

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
norm = mpl.colors.BoundaryNorm(np.linspace(-4, 4, 5), 256)
X = np.random.randn(10, 10)
pc = ax.imshow(X, cmap='RdBu_r', norm=norm)
plt.show()

Move the mouse cursor over the image and observe the crash.
Alternatively, create an AxesImage/ScalarMappable with a BoundaryNorm, then hover the cursor over the image in an interactive session.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Hovering the mouse over the image shows a formatted scalar value (with an appropriate number of significant digits) in the status/toolbar without raising an exception or stopping interaction.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Hovering the mouse over the image raises ValueError: "BoundaryNorm is not invertible" (propagating from BoundaryNorm.inverse) originating from Artist.format_cursor_data -> self.norm.inverse(...), causing the interactive cursor behavior to stop.

The flow in get_backend() in __init__ simply returns rcParams['backend'] which may trigger logic in RcParams.__getitem__ if key == 'backend' and self is global rcParams. RcParams.__getitem__ checks if backend is _auto_backend_sentinel and then imports pyplot and calls plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel). That resolves the auto backend by calling switch_backend. In _get_backend_mod, they explicitly mention switch_backend(dict.__getitem__(rcParams, "backend")) to avoid resolution. But get_backend uses rcParams['backend'], which will resolve sentinel. However in the bug reproduction, get_backend() is called. So rcParams['backend'] may call plt.switch_backend which in switch_backend begins by close("all"). close('all') calls Gcf.destroy_all which clears Gcf.figs. So it is expected that get_backend() can have this side effect only if rcParams['backend'] is the auto sentinel. Why would that delete figures created under rc_context but not others? The bug indicates that only when the first figure in Gcf.figs was created under rc_context, then get_backend clears all figures. That suggests that rc_context's finally block reverts rcParams, possibly setting rcParams['backend'] to something else (maybe removing backend or setting to sentinel) and causing resolution? Let's inspect rc_context: it copies orig = rcParams.copy(); then potentially updates rcParams, yield, finally dict.update(rcParams, orig) revert. Note: rcParams.copy() will create a RcParams instance with items. But RcParams.copy is inherited from dict.copy, which returns a shallow copy (a dict), but in this RcParams class no explicit copy method defined; earlier rcParamsOrig = rcParams.copy() used in other code. But in copy, keys like 'backend' may refer to sentinel or actual backend.

Let's reason through reproduction steps:

- Not starting interactive mode; rcParams likely has backend default sentinel? In global initialization, dict.update(rcParams, dict.items(rcParamsDefault)) where rcParamsDefault had backend set to _auto_backend_sentinel unless packager set else. So rcParams['backend'] is sentinel initially.

- Then with rc_context(): fig2 = plt.figure() creates a figure under modified rcParams; but rc_context when entering sets orig = rcParams.copy() capturing the sentinel perhaps. rc_context then applies whatever changes? In the reproduction they do with rc_context() without rc param; so entering rc_context() will 'orig = rcParams.copy()' then try: if fname: ... if rc: ... yield. So it doesn't change rcParams. On exit, finally dict.update(rcParams, orig) - but orig equals a copy of rcParams so update no-op. So rc_context() without modifications does nothing, but still creates context manager object and executes enter/exit.

So why would figure created inside rc_context have different effect? The context manager sets orig to rcParams.copy(). rcParams.copy() will call dict.copy which uses RcParams.copy? There's no copy method, but dictionaries have copy. But RcParams inherits from MutableMapping and dict, not overriding copy, so copy returns a shallow dict (not RcParams). rc_context later calls dict.update(rcParams, orig) in finally line 1098. That uses dict.update with rcParams as first arg? Wait rc_context finally uses dict.update(rcParams, orig) to revert. That is same as rcParams.update(orig) but using dict.update to bypass RcParams.__setitem__? Using dict.update prevents resolution side effects like setting backend with sentinel logic. But that's in rc_defaults earlier - same technique.

So where does the bug happen? Let's trace switch_backend: when rcParams['backend'] is sentinel, RcParams.__getitem__ will import pyplot as plt and call plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel). That will call switch_backend sentinel branch which calls close("all"). That will destroy all figures. So any call to get_backend() when rcParams['backend'] is sentinel will lead to closing all figures (as a consequence of selecting backend auto). But get_backend() is supposed to return name of current backend, but in some cases they preferred to not resolve sentinel? However they have _get_backend_mod which uses dict.__getitem__(rcParams, "backend") to avoid resolution in other code. But get_backend intentionally resolves? Maybe it's expected to resolve sentinel, but side effect of closing figures is surprising to user. Thought: Step 1 — classify the report
- Goal: find the likely places in the code to inspect (get_backend, rc_context, Gcf, switch_backend, close/destroy).
- Reasoning: the bug describes get_backend() clearing Gcf.figs, and mentions rc_context and figures created inside it; that suggests interaction between rcParams access and backend resolution (which may call switch_backend and close("all")). So I will start by locating get_backend, rc_context, and Gcf.

Tool call: classify_report
- Result: Identified relevant items: get_backend(), rc_context(), Gcf, Gcf.destroy_fig(), pyplot.figure(), plt.switch_backend(), OrderedDict for Gcf.figs. Good starting points.

Step 2 — find get_backend implementation
- Reasoning: to see what get_backend does and whether it can trigger side effects.

Tool call: get_file_context on lib/matplotlib/__init__.py (lines around get_backend)
- What I looked for: get_backend implementation and RcParams usage.
- What I learned:
  - get_backend() simply returns rcParams['backend'] (matplotlib.__init__.py, lines ~1170).
  - rcParams is an instance of RcParams (defined in same file).
  - RcParams.__getitem__ has a special-case for key == 'backend' when self is the global rcParams: if the stored value is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel, it imports pyplot and calls plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel). That triggers backend resolution and therefore switch_backend is invoked lazily on first access of the sentinel backend.

Step 3 — inspect RcParams.__getitem__
- Reasoning: accessing rcParams['backend'] is what get_backend() does; RcParams.__getitem__ may cause switch_backend when backend value is the auto-sentinel.

Tool call: get_file_context on lib/matplotlib/__init__.py (RcParams class)
- What I learned:
  - RcParams.__getitem__ (lines ~658-679) does:
    - If key == 'backend' and self is the global rcParams:
      - val = dict.__getitem__(self, key)
      - if val is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel:
          from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
          plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel)
      - then returns dict.__getitem__(self, key)
  - So get_backend() will invoke backend resolution (plt.switch_backend) if rcParams['backend'] is the auto-sentinel.

Step 4 — inspect switch_backend() and close('all')
- Reasoning: switch_backend may call close('all') to close existing figures; that would explain Gcf.figs being emptied.

Tool call: get_file_context on lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py (switch_backend)
- What I learned:
  - switch_backend(newbackend) starts by calling close("all") (pyplot.switch_backend, line ~225).
  - close("all") calls _pylab_helpers.Gcf.destroy_all(), which disconnects/destroys managers and clears Gcf.figs.
  - Thus, resolving the backend sentinel (leading to switch_backend) can clear all managed figures.

Step 5 — inspect rc_context implementation
- Reasoning: the bug states "figures created under rc_context"; I need to see what rc_context does and whether it reverts rcParams in a way that would make get_backend() trigger backend resolution (and thus clearing).

Tool call: get_file_context on lib/matplotlib/__init__.py (rc_context)
- What I learned:
  - rc_context is a contextmanager:
    - orig = rcParams.copy()
    - if fname: rc_file(fname)
    - if rc: rcParams.update(rc)
    - yield
    - finally: dict.update(rcParams, orig)  # Revert to the original rcs.
  - Note: orig is a plain dict (rcParams.copy() returns a dict), and they use dict.update(rcParams, orig) to restore rcParams without invoking RcParams.__setitem__ logic. This is consistent with avoiding side-effects while reverting.
  - Crucially, orig was captured before any actions inside the context (so if a backend was resolved inside the context, orig will still have the pre-context backend value — commonly rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).

Step 6 — reason about the failing scenario
- Reconstructed sequence that causes the bug:
  1. Before entering rc_context, rcParams['backend'] = rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel (no backend loaded).
  2. Enter rc_context(): orig = rcParams.copy() captures backend as sentinel.
  3. Inside context, create a figure via plt.figure(): this causes pyplot to load the backend (switch_backend) and set rcParams['backend'] to a concrete backend (e.g., 'qtagg'). Gcf.figs receives the new FigureManager.
  4. On exiting rc_context, finally: dict.update(rcParams, orig) restores rcParams to the original values, including setting 'backend' back to the auto-backend sentinel (because orig['backend'] == sentinel).
     - This now makes rcParams['backend'] = sentinel again even though a concrete backend had been selected while creating the figure.
  5. Later, the user calls matplotlib.get_backend() (which does rcParams['backend']). RcParams.__getitem__ finds backend is sentinel, so it triggers plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel) in order to resolve the sentinel; switch_backend() begins by calling close('all'), which destroys all figures (Gcf.figs cleared).
- This is exactly the reported behavior: a figure created under rc_context (i.e., the first figure in Gcf.figs created after entering rc_context) ends up being destroyed by a subsequent get_backend() call because exiting rc_context restored backend to the sentinel.

Step 7 — find other places in the code that do the same dict.update(rcParams, orig)
- Reasoning: make sure we update all the relevant locations, not just the obvious one.
- Tool calls: search for patterns dict.update(rcParams, self._orig) and similar
- What I learned:
  - matplotlib.__init__.py: rc_context uses dict.update(rcParams, orig) — must change.
  - matplotlib/pyplot.py: _xkcd.__exit__ uses dict.update(rcParams, self._orig) — needs the same treatment (xkcd uses rcParams.copy() in __init__ and reverts in __exit__).
  - I did not find other context managers using dict.update(rcParams, ...). (I searched for similar patterns.)

Root cause (concise)
- rc_context (and similar context managers that restore rcParams using a snapshot taken before entering the context) restore the 'backend' rcParam to its pre-context value (which is typically the auto-backend sentinel). If a figure is created inside the context, that figure causes matplotlib to resolve and set rcParams['backend'] to a concrete backend; but exiting the context restores 'backend' to the sentinel. Later, calling get_backend() tries to resolve the sentinel by calling plt.switch_backend(...), which invokes close("all") and destroys existing figures (empties Gcf.figs). Hence the surprising clearing of Gcf.figs when the first figure was created under rc_context.

Complete fix — overview
- Prevent context managers that revert rcParams from restoring the 'backend' key back to the auto-backend sentinel if it was resolved inside the context.
- Specifically:
  - Change matplotlib.rc_context (in lib/matplotlib/__init__.py) so that when it reverts rcParams it does NOT restore the 'backend' key if orig['backend'] is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel. In practice: remove 'backend' from the saved dict before updating rcParams in that case.
  - Make the same change to pyplot._xkcd.__exit__ (lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py) which uses the same pattern.

Why this is correct
- Restoring 'backend' to the auto-backend sentinel loses the fact that a concrete backend has been loaded and results in unexpected backend re-resolution side-effects (close("all")). The backend is not a styling parameter and reverting it to the pre-context sentinel is surprising and dangerous when figures were created under the context. The fix preserves any backend that was selected/resolved during the context, while continuing to revert other rcParams as intended.

Exact code changes (the complete set of edits required)

1) File: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py
- Replace the final lines of rc_context (old code):

    finally:
        dict.update(rcParams, orig)  # Revert to the original rcs.

- With this (new code):

    finally:
        # Do not restore the 'backend' key to the auto-backend sentinel.
        # If the backend was resolved inside the context (e.g., by creating a
        # figure), restoring the sentinel would cause later accesses to
        # rcParams['backend'] (e.g., matplotlib.get_backend()) to try to
        # resolve it again, which calls switch_backend and will close all
        # existing figures.  To avoid this surprising behavior, if the
        # original backend was the auto sentinel, do not revert the backend.
        from . import rcsetup
        orig_backend = orig.get("backend", rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel)
        if orig_backend is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel:
            tmp = orig.copy()
            tmp.pop("backend", None)
            dict.update(rcParams, tmp)
        else:
            dict.update(rcParams, orig)

Notes:
- We use dict.update to restore without triggering RcParams.__setitem__ special-cases.
- We detect the case orig_backend is the sentinel using 'is' (consistent with sentinel identity checks elsewhere).
- If orig.backend is sentinel, we remove backend from the snapshot before updating; that leaves the current rcParams['backend'] intact (i.e., the concrete backend set during the context).

2) File: lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py
- Replace the __exit__ in class _xkcd that currently reads:

    def __exit__(self, *args):
        dict.update(rcParams, self._orig)

- With the same protection (pyplot already imports rcsetup at top), e.g.:

    def __exit__(self, *args):
        # As in matplotlib.rc_context, do not restore the 'backend' to the
        # auto-backend sentinel if that was the original setting.
        orig_backend = self._orig.get("backend", rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel)
        if orig_backend is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel:
            tmp = self._orig.copy()
            tmp.pop("backend", None)
            dict.update(rcParams, tmp)
        else:
            dict.update(rcParams, self._orig)

(If you prefer, import rcsetup locally via from matplotlib import rcsetup, but rcsetup is already imported in pyplot.py.)

Rationale for also changing pyplot._xkcd
- The xkcd context manager uses the same pattern (save rcParams.copy() then dict.update on exit). While it may not be commonly used to create figures that cause backend resolution, it follows the same pattern and therefore is vulnerable to the same issue. For completeness we must adjust all contexts that use the copy/restore pattern; I found _xkcd as another such location and patched it as well.

Other locations considered & why not changed
- The initial module-level use of dict.update to populate rcParams from defaults (during import) is intended initialization and not a context revert; it should remain as-is.
- There were no other context managers using dict.update(rcParams, ...) found in the repository at the time of this investigation. If any other context managers are added in the future that capture and restore rcParams with dict.update, they should follow the same policy for 'backend'.

Edge cases and alternatives
- Alternative approach: treat 'backend' specially in RcParams.__setitem__ or __getitem__ so that restoring to sentinel doesn't cause a close("all") on later access. I judged that more invasive and likely to have wider implications; the least invasive and most localized change is to avoid reverting 'backend' to the sentinel in contexts that explicitly save/restore rcParams.
- If a caller intentionally wanted to restore backend to the auto sentinel (rare), this change prevents that when orig had sentinel. But restoring backend to sentinel is itself problematic: it makes backends lazy again and can trigger switch_backend later. Restoring 'backend' to sentinel is not generally useful in contexts that temporarily change style parameters, so the change is safe.

Testing suggestions
- Add unit test covering the reproduction scenario:
  - Ensure initial rcParams['backend'] is auto sentinel (or set it to sentinel for the test).
  - Do with rc_context(): create a figure (via plt.figure()).
  - After exiting rc_context, ensure rcParams['backend'] remains the concrete backend chosen (or at least does not revert to sentinel).
  - Ensure a subsequent call to matplotlib.get_backend() does not clear Gcf.figs.
- Add similar test ensuring that if orig.backend was a concrete backend, it is restored correctly on exit.

Summary of files to change (complete list)
- lib/matplotlib/__init__.py
  - Modify the finally block in rc_context to avoid restoring 'backend' when orig had the auto-backend sentinel.
- lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py
  - Modify _xkcd.__exit__ to do the same when restoring its saved rcParams snapshot.

If you want, I can:
- Produce an exact patch/diff (unified diff) covering both files.
- Add a minimal unit test to go into the test suite reproducing the original short reproduction and asserting no clearing occurs.
- Run through further code search to ensure no other rc-context-like constructs need the same guard (I searched for dict.update(rcParams, ...), dict.update(rcParams, self._orig), and rcParams.copy() usage and did not find others that both copy and later dict.update).

bug_report.Title: matplotlib.get_backend() can clear Gcf.figs when a figure was created inside rc_context
bug_report.Description: Calling matplotlib.get_backend() can remove existing figures from the global figure manager (plt._pylab_helpers.Gcf.figs) when the first figure in Gcf.figs was created inside an rc_context() block.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) rc_context() restores rcParams from a snapshot taken at context entry. Location: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, function rc_context. Implementation captures orig = rcParams.copy() at entry and then does dict.update(rcParams, orig) in the finally block to revert settings. Because orig was taken before any backend resolution inside the context, this can restore the 'backend' rcParam back to the auto-backend sentinel (rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).

2) get_backend() simply returns rcParams['backend'], and RcParams.__getitem__ lazily resolves the auto-backend sentinel by calling pyplot.switch_backend(...). Location: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, function get_backend and class RcParams.__getitem__. The exact behavior: if dict.__getitem__(rcParams, 'backend') is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel, RcParams.__getitem__ imports matplotlib.pyplot and calls plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).

3) switch_backend(...) begins by closing all figures. Location: lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py, function switch_backend (and close('all')). The sequence: plt.switch_backend(...) calls close("all"), which calls _pylab_helpers.Gcf.destroy_all(), which clears the OrderedDict of managers (Gcf.figs). Therefore resolving the sentinel via get_backend() can cause all figures to be destroyed.

4) Interaction that triggers the bug (relationship preserved):
   - orig = rcParams.copy() (rc_context entry)
   - inside context: creating a figure causes backend resolution and sets rcParams['backend'] to a concrete backend
   - exiting context: dict.update(rcParams, orig) restores 'backend' to rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel (because orig captured the sentinel)
   - later: get_backend() -> rcParams['backend'] is sentinel -> RcParams.__getitem__ calls plt.switch_backend(...) -> switch_backend calls close("all") -> Gcf.figs cleared.

5) The same snapshot/restore pattern appears in pyplot._xkcd and similarly reverts rcParams using dict.update(rcParams, self._orig), so it is also susceptible. Location: lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py, class _xkcd.__exit__.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Calling matplotlib.get_backend() should not remove or destroy figures that were already created and managed by the global figure manager (Gcf). Figures created inside an rc_context should remain in Gcf.figs after leaving the context and after calling get_backend().
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: If the first (or only) figure in Gcf.figs was created inside an rc_context, calling matplotlib.get_backend() can result in Gcf.figs becoming empty (figures are removed). Consequences: operations that expect the figure to be managed (e.g., plt.close(fig)) may fail because the figure manager has been destroyed/removed from Gcf.

        if rasterized and not hasattr(self.draw, "_supports_rasterization"):

        """

        self.stale = True

    def draw(self, renderer):
        """
        Draw the Artist (and its children) using the given renderer.

        This has no effect if the artist is not visible (`.Artist.get_visible`
        returns False).

        Parameters
        ----------
        renderer : `.RendererBase` subclass.

        Notes
        -----
        This method is overridden in the Artist subclasses.
        """
        if not self.get_visible():
            return
        self.stale = False

    def set_alpha(self, alpha):
        """
        Set the alpha value used for blending - not supported on all backends.

        Parameters
        ----------
        alpha : scalar or None
            *alpha* must be within the 0-1 range, inclusive.
        """
        if alpha is not None and not isinstance(alpha, Number):
            raise TypeError(
                f'alpha must be numeric or None, not {type(alpha)}')
        if alpha is not None and not (0 <= alpha <= 1):
            raise ValueError(f'alpha ({alpha}) is outside 0-1 range')
        self._alpha = alpha
        self.pchanged()
        self.stale = True

    def _set_alpha_for_array(self, alpha):
        """
        Set the alpha value used for blending - not supported on all backends.

        Parameters
        ----------
        alpha : array-like or scalar or None
            All values must be within the 0-1 range, inclusive.
            Masked values and nans are not supported.
        """
        if isinstance(alpha, str):
            raise TypeError("alpha must be numeric or None, not a string")
        if not np.iterable(alpha):
            Artist.set_alpha(self, alpha)
            return
        alpha = np.asarray(alpha)
        if not (0 <= alpha.min() and alpha.max() <= 1):
            raise ValueError('alpha must be between 0 and 1, inclusive, '
                             f'but min is {alpha.min()}, max is {alpha.max()}')
        self._alpha = alpha
        self.pchanged()
        self.stale = True

    def set_visible(self, b):
        """
        Set the artist's visibility.

        Parameters
        ----------
        b : bool
        """
        self._visible = b
        self.pchanged()
        self.stale = True

    def set_animated(self, b):
        """
        Set whether the artist is intended to be used in an animation.

        If True, the artist is excluded from regular drawing of the figure.
        You have to call `.Figure.draw_artist` / `.Axes.draw_artist`
        explicitly on the artist. This approach is used to speed up animations
        using blitting.

        See also `matplotlib.animation` and
        :doc:`/tutorials/advanced/blitting`.

        Parameters
        ----------
        b : bool
        """
        if self._animated != b:
            self._animated = b
            self.pchanged()

import matplotlib.colors as mcolors

import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

        ymax = max(title.get_position()[1] for title in titles)
        for title in titles:
            # now line up all the titles at the highest baseline.
            x, _ = title.get_position()
            title.set_position((x, ymax))

    @martist.allow_rasterization
    def draw(self, renderer):

            raise RuntimeError('No renderer defined')

            return

        renderer.open_group('axes', gid=self.get_gid())

        # prevent triggering call backs during the draw process
        self._stale = True

        # loop over self and child Axes...
        locator = self.get_axes_locator()
        if locator:
            pos = locator(self, renderer)
            self.apply_aspect(pos)
        else:
            self.apply_aspect()

        artists = self.get_children()
        artists.remove(self.patch)

        # the frame draws the edges around the Axes patch -- we
        # decouple these so the patch can be in the background and the
        # frame in the foreground. Do this before drawing the axis
        # objects so that the spine has the opportunity to update them.
        if not (self.axison and self._frameon):
            for spine in self.spines.values():
                artists.remove(spine)

        if not self.axison:
            for _axis in self._axis_map.values():
                artists.remove(_axis)

        if not self.figure.canvas.is_saving():
            artists = [
                a for a in artists
                if not a.get_animated() or isinstance(a, mimage.AxesImage)]
        artists = sorted(artists, key=attrgetter('zorder'))

"""
`matplotlib.figure` implements the following classes:

`Figure`
    Top level `~matplotlib.artist.Artist`, which holds all plot elements.
    Many methods are implemented in `FigureBase`.

`SubFigure`
    A logical figure inside a figure, usually added to a figure (or parent
    `SubFigure`) with `Figure.add_subfigure` or `Figure.subfigures` methods
    (provisional API v3.4).

`SubplotParams`
    Control the default spacing between subplots.
"""

from contextlib import ExitStack

import matplotlib as mpl
from matplotlib import _blocking_input, _docstring, projections
from matplotlib.artist import (
    Artist, allow_rasterization, _finalize_rasterization)

    FigureCanvasBase, NonGuiException, MouseButton, _get_renderer)
import matplotlib._api as _api

                                   TransformedBbox)

_log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def _stale_figure_callback(self, val):
    if self.figure:
        self.figure.stale = val


class _AxesStack:
    """
    Helper class to track axes in a figure.

    Axes are tracked both in the order in which they have been added
    (``self._axes`` insertion/iteration order) and in the separate "gca" stack
    (which is the index to which they map in the ``self._axes`` dict).
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self._axes = {}  # Mapping of axes to "gca" order.
        self._counter = itertools.count()

    def as_list(self):
        """List the axes that have been added to the figure."""
        return [*self._axes]  # This relies on dict preserving order.

    def remove(self, a):
        """Remove the axes from the stack."""
        self._axes.pop(a)

    def bubble(self, a):
        """Move an axes, which must already exist in the stack, to the top."""
        if a not in self._axes:
            raise ValueError("Axes has not been added yet")
        self._axes[a] = next(self._counter)

    def add(self, a):
        """Add an axes to the stack, ignoring it if already present."""
        if a not in self._axes:
            self._axes[a] = next(self._counter)

    def current(self):
        """Return the active axes, or None if the stack is empty."""
        return max(self._axes, key=self._axes.__getitem__, default=None)


class SubplotParams:
    """
    A class to hold the parameters for a subplot.
    """

    def __init__(self, left=None, bottom=None, right=None, top=None,
                 wspace=None, hspace=None):
        """
        Defaults are given by :rc:`figure.subplot.[name]`.

        Parameters
        ----------
        left : float
            The position of the left edge of the subplots,
            as a fraction of the figure width.
        right : float
            The position of the right edge of the subplots,
            as a fraction of the figure width.
        bottom : float
            The position of the bottom edge of the subplots,
            as a fraction of the figure height.
        top : float
            The position of the top edge of the subplots,
            as a fraction of the figure height.
        wspace : float
            The width of the padding between subplots,
            as a fraction of the average Axes width.
        hspace : float
            The height of the padding between subplots,
            as a fraction of the average Axes height.
        """
        self._validate = True
        for key in ["left", "bottom", "right", "top", "wspace", "hspace"]:
            setattr(self, key, mpl.rcParams[f"figure.subplot.{key}"])
        self.update(left, bottom, right, top, wspace, hspace)

    # Also remove _validate after deprecation elapses.
    validate = _api.deprecate_privatize_attribute("3.5")

    def update(self, left=None, bottom=None, right=None, top=None,
               wspace=None, hspace=None):
        """
        Update the dimensions of the passed parameters. *None* means unchanged.
        """
        if self._validate:
            if ((left if left is not None else self.left)
                    >= (right if right is not None else self.right)):
                raise ValueError('left cannot be >= right')
            if ((bottom if bottom is not None else self.bottom)
                    >= (top if top is not None else self.top)):
                raise ValueError('bottom cannot be >= top')
        if left is not None:
            self.left = left
        if right is not None:
            self.right = right
        if bottom is not None:
            self.bottom = bottom
        if top is not None:
            self.top = top
        if wspace is not None:
            self.wspace = wspace
        if hspace is not None:
            self.hspace = hspace

    Base class for `.Figure` and `.SubFigure` containing the methods that add
    artists to the figure or subfigure, create Axes, etc.

        self.figure = self

        self._localaxes = []  # track all axes
        self.artists = []
        self.lines = []
        self.patches = []
        self.texts = []

        """Also runs apply_aspect"""
        artists = self.get_children()
        for sfig in self.subfigs:
            artists.remove(sfig)
            childa = sfig.get_children()
            for child in childa:
                if child in artists:
                    artists.remove(child)

        artists.remove(self.patch)
        artists = sorted(
            (artist for artist in artists if not artist.get_animated()),
            key=lambda artist: artist.get_zorder())

            locator = ax.get_axes_locator()
            if locator:
                pos = locator(ax, renderer)
                ax.apply_aspect(pos)
            else:
                ax.apply_aspect()

            for child in ax.get_children():
                if hasattr(child, 'apply_aspect'):
                    locator = child.get_axes_locator()
                    if locator:
                        pos = locator(child, renderer)
                        child.apply_aspect(pos)
                    else:

    def autofmt_xdate(
            self, bottom=0.2, rotation=30, ha='right', which='major'):
        """
        Date ticklabels often overlap, so it is useful to rotate them
        and right align them.  Also, a common use case is a number of
        subplots with shared x-axis where the x-axis is date data.  The
        ticklabels are often long, and it helps to rotate them on the
        bottom subplot and turn them off on other subplots, as well as
        turn off xlabels.

        Parameters
        ----------
        bottom : float, default: 0.2
            The bottom of the subplots for `subplots_adjust`.
        rotation : float, default: 30 degrees
            The rotation angle of the xtick labels in degrees.
        ha : {'left', 'center', 'right'}, default: 'right'
            The horizontal alignment of the xticklabels.
        which : {'major', 'minor', 'both'}, default: 'major'
            Selects which ticklabels to rotate.
        """
        _api.check_in_list(['major', 'minor', 'both'], which=which)
        allsubplots = all(hasattr(ax, 'get_subplotspec') for ax in self.axes)
        if len(self.axes) == 1:
            for label in self.axes[0].get_xticklabels(which=which):
                label.set_ha(ha)
                label.set_rotation(rotation)
        else:
            if allsubplots:
                for ax in self.get_axes():
                    if ax.get_subplotspec().is_last_row():
                        for label in ax.get_xticklabels(which=which):
                            label.set_ha(ha)
                            label.set_rotation(rotation)
                    else:
                        for label in ax.get_xticklabels(which=which):
                            label.set_visible(False)

            self.subplots_adjust(bottom=bottom)

default: %(va)s

# art3d.py, original mplot3d version by John Porter
# Parts rewritten by Reinier Heeres <reinier@heeres.eu>
# Minor additions by Ben Axelrod <baxelrod@coroware.com>

"""
Module containing 3D artist code and functions to convert 2D
artists into 3D versions which can be added to an Axes3D.
"""

import math

import numpy as np

from matplotlib import (
    artist, cbook, colors as mcolors, lines, text as mtext, path as mpath)
from matplotlib.collections import (
    LineCollection, PolyCollection, PatchCollection, PathCollection)
from matplotlib.colors import Normalize
from matplotlib.patches import Patch
from . import proj3d


def _norm_angle(a):
    """Return the given angle normalized to -180 < *a* <= 180 degrees."""
    a = (a + 360) % 360
    if a > 180:
        a = a - 360
    return a


def _norm_text_angle(a):
    """Return the given angle normalized to -90 < *a* <= 90 degrees."""
    a = (a + 180) % 180
    if a > 90:
        a = a - 180
    return a


def get_dir_vector(zdir):
    """
    Return a direction vector.

    Parameters
    ----------
    zdir : {'x', 'y', 'z', None, 3-tuple}
        The direction. Possible values are:

        - 'x': equivalent to (1, 0, 0)
        - 'y': equivalent to (0, 1, 0)
        - 'z': equivalent to (0, 0, 1)
        - *None*: equivalent to (0, 0, 0)
        - an iterable (x, y, z) is converted to a NumPy array, if not already

    Returns
    -------
    x, y, z : array-like
        The direction vector.
    """
    if zdir == 'x':
        return np.array((1, 0, 0))
    elif zdir == 'y':
        return np.array((0, 1, 0))
    elif zdir == 'z':
        return np.array((0, 0, 1))
    elif zdir is None:
        return np.array((0, 0, 0))
    elif np.iterable(zdir) and len(zdir) == 3:
        return np.array(zdir)
    else:
        raise ValueError("'x', 'y', 'z', None or vector of length 3 expected")


class Text3D(mtext.Text):
    """
    Text object with 3D position and direction.

    Parameters
    ----------
    x, y, z
        The position of the text.
    text : str
        The text string to display.
    zdir : {'x', 'y', 'z', None, 3-tuple}
        The direction of the text. See `.get_dir_vector` for a description of
        the values.

    Other Parameters
    ----------------
    **kwargs
         All other parameters are passed on to `~matplotlib.text.Text`.
   """

    def __init__(self, x=0, y=0, z=0, text='', zdir='z', **kwargs):
        mtext.Text.__init__(self, x, y, text, **kwargs)
        self.set_3d_properties(z, zdir)

    def get_position_3d(self):
        """Return the (x, y, z) position of the text."""
        return self._x, self._y, self._z

    def set_position_3d(self, xyz, zdir=None):
        """
        Set the (*x*, *y*, *z*) position of the text.

        Parameters
        ----------
        xyz : (float, float, float)
            The position in 3D space.
        zdir : {'x', 'y', 'z', None, 3-tuple}
            The direction of the text. If unspecified, the zdir will not be
            changed.
        """
        super().set_position(xyz[:2])
        self.set_z(xyz[2])
        if zdir is not None:
            self._dir_vec = get_dir_vector(zdir)

    def set_z(self, z):
        """
        Set the *z* position of the text.

        Parameters
        ----------
        z : float
        """
        self._z = z
        self.stale = True

    def set_3d_properties(self, z=0, zdir='z'):
        self._z = z
        self._dir_vec = get_dir_vector(zdir)
        self.stale = True

    @artist.allow_rasterization
    def draw(self, renderer):
        position3d = np.array((self._x, self._y, self._z))
        proj = proj3d.proj_trans_points(
            [position3d, position3d + self._dir_vec], self.axes.M)
        dx = proj[0][1] - proj[0][0]
        dy = proj[1][1] - proj[1][0]
        angle = math.degrees(math.atan2(dy, dx))
        with cbook._setattr_cm(self, _x=proj[0][0], _y=proj[1][0],
                               _rotation=_norm_text_angle(angle)):
            mtext.Text.draw(self, renderer)
        self.stale = False

    def get_tightbbox(self, renderer=None):
        # Overwriting the 2d Text behavior which is not valid for 3d.
        # For now, just return None to exclude from layout calculation.
        return None


def text_2d_to_3d(obj, z=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a Text to a Text3D object."""
    obj.__class__ = Text3D
    obj.set_3d_properties(z, zdir)


class Line3D(lines.Line2D):
    """
    3D line object.
    """

    def __init__(self, xs, ys, zs, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Keyword arguments are passed onto :func:`~matplotlib.lines.Line2D`.
        """
        super().__init__([], [], *args, **kwargs)
        self._verts3d = xs, ys, zs

    def set_3d_properties(self, zs=0, zdir='z'):
        xs = self.get_xdata()
        ys = self.get_ydata()
        zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs))
        self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir)
        self.stale = True

    def set_data_3d(self, *args):
        """
        Set the x, y and z data

        Parameters
        ----------
        x : array-like
            The x-data to be plotted.
        y : array-like
            The y-data to be plotted.
        z : array-like
            The z-data to be plotted.

        Notes
        -----
        Accepts x, y, z arguments or a single array-like (x, y, z)
        """
        if len(args) == 1:
            self._verts3d = args[0]
        else:
            self._verts3d = args
        self.stale = True

    def get_data_3d(self):
        """
        Get the current data

        Returns
        -------
        verts3d : length-3 tuple or array-like
            The current data as a tuple or array-like.
        """
        return self._verts3d

    @artist.allow_rasterization
    def draw(self, renderer):
        xs3d, ys3d, zs3d = self._verts3d
        xs, ys, zs = proj3d.proj_transform(xs3d, ys3d, zs3d, self.axes.M)
        self.set_data(xs, ys)
        super().draw(renderer)
        self.stale = False


def line_2d_to_3d(line, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a 2D line to 3D."""

    line.__class__ = Line3D
    line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir)


def _path_to_3d_segment(path, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a path to a 3D segment."""

    zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(path))
    pathsegs = path.iter_segments(simplify=False, curves=False)
    seg = [(x, y, z) for (((x, y), code), z) in zip(pathsegs, zs)]
    seg3d = [juggle_axes(x, y, z, zdir) for (x, y, z) in seg]
    return seg3d


def _paths_to_3d_segments(paths, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert paths from a collection object to 3D segments."""

    if not np.iterable(zs):
        zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(paths))
    else:
        if len(zs) != len(paths):
            raise ValueError('Number of z-coordinates does not match paths.')

    segs = [_path_to_3d_segment(path, pathz, zdir)
            for path, pathz in zip(paths, zs)]
    return segs


def _path_to_3d_segment_with_codes(path, zs=0, zdir='z'):
    """Convert a path to a 3D segment with path codes."""

    zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(path))
    pathsegs = path.iter_segments(simplify=False, curves=False)
    seg_codes = [((x, y, z), code) for ((x, y), code), z in zip(pathsegs, zs)]
    if seg_codes:
        seg, codes = zip(*seg_codes)
        seg3d = [juggle_axes(x, y, z, zdir) for (x, y, z) in seg]

import matplotlib as mpl
from matplotlib import cbook
from matplotlib.axes._axes import Axes
from matplotlib.gridspec import SubplotSpec


class SubplotBase:
    """
    Base class for subplots, which are :class:`Axes` instances with
    additional methods to facilitate generating and manipulating a set
    of :class:`Axes` within a figure.
    """

    def __init__(self, fig, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        fig : `matplotlib.figure.Figure`

        *args : tuple (*nrows*, *ncols*, *index*) or int
            The array of subplots in the figure has dimensions ``(nrows,
            ncols)``, and *index* is the index of the subplot being created.
            *index* starts at 1 in the upper left corner and increases to the
            right.

            If *nrows*, *ncols*, and *index* are all single digit numbers, then
            *args* can be passed as a single 3-digit number (e.g. 234 for
            (2, 3, 4)).

        **kwargs
            Keyword arguments are passed to the Axes (sub)class constructor.
        """
        # _axes_class is set in the subplot_class_factory
        self._axes_class.__init__(self, fig, [0, 0, 1, 1], **kwargs)
        # This will also update the axes position.
        self.set_subplotspec(SubplotSpec._from_subplot_args(fig, args))

    def get_subplotspec(self):
        """Return the `.SubplotSpec` instance associated with the subplot."""
        return self._subplotspec

    def set_subplotspec(self, subplotspec):
        """Set the `.SubplotSpec`. instance associated with the subplot."""
        self._subplotspec = subplotspec
        self._set_position(subplotspec.get_position(self.figure))

    def get_gridspec(self):
        """Return the `.GridSpec` instance associated with the subplot."""
        return self._subplotspec.get_gridspec()

    def label_outer(self):
        """
        Only show "outer" labels and tick labels.

        x-labels are only kept for subplots on the last row (or first row, if
        labels are on the top side); y-labels only for subplots on the first
        column (or last column, if labels are on the right side).
        """
        self._label_outer_xaxis(check_patch=False)
        self._label_outer_yaxis(check_patch=False)

    def _label_outer_xaxis(self, *, check_patch):
        # see documentation in label_outer.
        if check_patch and not isinstance(self.patch, mpl.patches.Rectangle):
            return
        ss = self.get_subplotspec()
        label_position = self.xaxis.get_label_position()
        if not ss.is_first_row():  # Remove top label/ticklabels/offsettext.
            if label_position == "top":
                self.set_xlabel("")
            self.xaxis.set_tick_params(which="both", labeltop=False)
            if self.xaxis.offsetText.get_position()[1] == 1:
                self.xaxis.offsetText.set_visible(False)
        if not ss.is_last_row():  # Remove bottom label/ticklabels/offsettext.
            if label_position == "bottom":
                self.set_xlabel("")
            self.xaxis.set_tick_params(which="both", labelbottom=False)
            if self.xaxis.offsetText.get_position()[1] == 0:
                self.xaxis.offsetText.set_visible(False)

    def _label_outer_yaxis(self, *, check_patch):
        # see documentation in label_outer.
        if check_patch and not isinstance(self.patch, mpl.patches.Rectangle):
            return
        ss = self.get_subplotspec()
        label_position = self.yaxis.get_label_position()
        if not ss.is_first_col():  # Remove left label/ticklabels/offsettext.
            if label_position == "left":
                self.set_ylabel("")
            self.yaxis.set_tick_params(which="both", labelleft=False)
            if self.yaxis.offsetText.get_position()[0] == 0:
                self.yaxis.offsetText.set_visible(False)
        if not ss.is_last_col():  # Remove right label/ticklabels/offsettext.
            if label_position == "right":
                self.set_ylabel("")
            self.yaxis.set_tick_params(which="both", labelright=False)
            if self.yaxis.offsetText.get_position()[0] == 1:
                self.yaxis.offsetText.set_visible(False)

    def _make_twin_axes(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Make a twinx axes of self. This is used for twinx and twiny."""
        if 'sharex' in kwargs and 'sharey' in kwargs:
            # The following line is added in v2.2 to avoid breaking Seaborn,
            # which currently uses this internal API.
            if kwargs["sharex"] is not self and kwargs["sharey"] is not self:
                raise ValueError("Twinned Axes may share only one axis")
        twin = self.figure.add_subplot(self.get_subplotspec(), *args, **kwargs)
        self.set_adjustable('datalim')
        twin.set_adjustable('datalim')
        self._twinned_axes.join(self, twin)
        return twin


subplot_class_factory = cbook._make_class_factory(
    SubplotBase, "{}Subplot", "_axes_class")
Subplot = subplot_class_factory(Axes)  # Provided for backward compatibility.
